passages.title: The Boat That Wouldn't Quit
passages.subject: ELA
passages.text:
My brother Mateo won the Mill Creek junk boat race three years in a row. Everyone in town still remembered his silver boat shaped like a shark, with fins that actually wiggled as it sliced down the creek. So when I, June Reyes, signed up for this year's race, people smiled at me the way you smile at a puppy trying to climb a tall set of stairs. "Another Reyes boat," they said. "This should be good."

Here is the thing nobody knew: I did not have a plan for fins that wiggled. I had a plan for not sinking.

The rules of the race were simple. You built a boat out of junk, you set it in Mill Creek, and you let it float down past the old stone bridge. The last boat still floating at the finish line was the winner. Mateo's boats had always been the fastest and the flashiest on the water. Mine was going to be neither.

For two whole weeks, I collected things other people had thrown away: bottle caps, a cracked plastic cutting board, a tangle of fishing line, and four empty milk jugs that still smelled a little like milk. I lashed the milk jugs to the four corners of the cutting board, two in front and two in back, so the weight stayed even. Then I tested it in the bathtub. I pushed it. I poured water on it. I set my little sister's toy frog on top to see if it could carry weight. Eleven times I tested that boat, and eleven times it floated.

Mateo watched me tie the last knot and raised one eyebrow. "That's it?" he asked. "No sail? No paint? Not even a flag?"

"It floats," I said.

"It looks like a sandwich," he said. He wasn't trying to be mean. It really did look like a sandwich — a flat board with four lumpy jugs for bread.

On race day, the creek bank was packed with people and boats, and every single boat was more beautiful than mine. There was a dragon covered in paper scales, a rocket wrapped in shining foil, and a tall ship with a real cloth sail that puffed out in the wind like a proud chest. Next to all of them, my milk-jug sandwich looked like a mistake somebody had left behind. My face went hot, and for one long second I almost picked my boat back up and walked home.

But then the whistle blew, and there was no more time to feel embarrassed.

The boats hit the water all at once. The dragon shot out ahead, fast and fierce. The foil rocket spun in a cheerful circle and then kept spinning, going absolutely nowhere. The tall ship caught the wind and flew across the water — until a sudden gust tipped it sideways, and its proud sail dragged it straight under. The crowd groaned.

My sandwich just floated. Slow. Steady. A little boring. It did not race and it did not show off. It bumped gently along the edge of the creek as if it were in no hurry at all.

One by one, the fancy boats began to fail. The dragon's paper scales soaked through, grew heavy, and folded the boat in half. A boat built from soda cans slowly filled with water and sank with a sad little gurgle. Near the stone bridge, where the water ran quick and rough over the rocks, three more boats flipped and disappeared. Every time one went down, the crowd gasped, and every time, I held my breath and watched my slow little sandwich keep bumping along.

When my boat finally slid into the rough water under the bridge, I squeezed my eyes shut. I was certain those bumps would be the end of it. But when I opened my eyes, it was still there — riding over the choppy water like the bumps were nothing at all, four milk jugs holding it up like four small, stubborn hands.

"Go, sandwich!" a voice yelled from the bank. Then more people joined in. "Go, sandwich! Go!" Even Mateo was cupping his hands around his mouth and shouting it.

By the time my boat drifted past the finish line, it was the only one left on the water.

I did not win because my boat was fast. I won because it was the last one floating — because I had spent two weeks making sure it would not sink, while everyone else was busy making sure their boats would look amazing.

Mateo lifted my dripping milk-jug sandwich over his head like a trophy. "Three years I tried to build the fastest boat," he said, grinning at me. "You went and built the smartest one."

I used to think a winning boat had to be the flashiest thing on the water. Now I know something better. Sometimes the boat that wins is simply the one that was built to last.

I am already saving milk jugs for next year.

textFeaturesJson.featureText: four milk jugs holding it up like four small, stubborn hands

items.subject: ELA
items.studentFacingPrompt: What is the most important lesson June learns in the story?
answerChoicesJson: A boat needs milk jugs to float.
People should not throw away useful things.
Careful work matters more than fancy looks.
Younger sisters should test boats before a race.

structuredChoicesJson.text: A boat needs milk jugs to float.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Too narrow: this detail describes June's boat, not the story's lesson.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: four milk jugs holding it up like four small, stubborn hands.

structuredChoicesJson.text: People should not throw away useful things.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Too narrow: reusing useful things is one action June takes, not the story's message.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: For two whole weeks, I collected things other people had thrown away: bottle caps, a cracked plastic cutting board, a tangle of fishing line, and four empty milk jugs that still smelled a little like milk.

structuredChoicesJson.text: Careful work matters more than fancy looks.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Correct.

structuredChoicesJson.text: Younger sisters should test boats before a race.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Unsupported inference: June tests the boat with a toy frog, not with her sister helping.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: I set my little sister's toy frog on top to see if it could carry weight.

responseSpecJson.prompt: What is the most important lesson June learns in the story?
choices: A boat needs milk jugs to float.
People should not throw away useful things.
Careful work matters more than fancy looks.
Younger sisters should test boats before a race.

structuredChoicesJson.text: A boat needs milk jugs to float.

items.subject: ELA
items.studentFacingPrompt: How do June's feelings about her boat change from the beginning to the end of the story?
answerChoicesJson: She is proud of the milk-jug sandwich at first, but ashamed after the race.
She stays bored as the slow sandwich bumps along the creek.
She is afraid near the bridge, but forgets the boat after Mateo lifts it.
She is embarrassed by it at first, but proud of it by the end.

structuredChoicesJson.text: She is proud of the milk-jug sandwich at first, but ashamed after the race.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Opposite claim: June is embarrassed at first, and her boat wins by the end.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: By the time my boat drifted past the finish line, it was the only one left on the water.

structuredChoicesJson.text: She stays bored as the slow sandwich bumps along the creek.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Unsupported inference: June worries and later reflects; she is not bored by the boat.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: When my boat finally slid into the rough water under the bridge, I squeezed my eyes shut.

structuredChoicesJson.text: She is afraid near the bridge, but forgets the boat after Mateo lifts it.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Wrong emphasis: June is embarrassed, not afraid of the boat, and she is already saving milk jugs for next year.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: I am already saving milk jugs for next year.

structuredChoicesJson.text: She is embarrassed by it at first, but proud of it by the end.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: June feels embarrassed beside the flashy boats, then understands why her carefully built boat won.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: my milk-jug sandwich looked like a mistake somebody had left behind.
My face went hot, and for one long second I almost picked my boat back up and walked home.
I did not win because my boat was fast.
I won because it was the last one floating — because I had spent two weeks making sure it would not sink, while everyone else was busy making sure their boats would look amazing.

responseSpecJson.prompt: How do June's feelings about her boat change from the beginning to the end of the story?
choices: She is proud of the milk-jug sandwich at first, but ashamed after the race.
She stays bored as the slow sandwich bumps along the creek.
She is afraid near the bridge, but forgets the boat after Mateo lifts it.
She is embarrassed by it at first, but proud of it by the end.

items.subject: ELA
items.studentFacingPrompt: From whose point of view is the story told?
answerChoicesJson: June, who built the milk-jug boat.
Mateo, June's older brother.
Someone watching from the creek bank.
The person who built the tall ship.

structuredChoicesJson.text: June, who built the milk-jug boat.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: The narrator uses first-person words and describes June's own actions and feelings.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: So when I, June Reyes, signed up for this year's race, people smiled at me the way you smile at a puppy trying to climb a tall set of stairs.
My face went hot, and for one long second I almost picked my boat back up and walked home.

structuredChoicesJson.text: Mateo, June's older brother.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Plausible misreading: Mateo is important in the story, but he is not the narrator.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: Mateo lifted my dripping milk-jug sandwich over his head like a trophy.

structuredChoicesJson.text: Someone watching from the creek bank.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Unsupported inference: people on the bank cheer, but the narrator tells June's own thoughts.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: a voice yelled from the bank.

structuredChoicesJson.text: The person who built the tall ship.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Wrong section: the tall ship is another boat in the race, not the narrator's boat.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: The tall ship caught the wind and flew across the water — until a sudden gust tipped it sideways, and its proud sail dragged it straight under.

responseSpecJson.prompt: From whose point of view is the story told?
choices: June, who built the milk-jug boat.

items.subject: ELA
items.studentFacingPrompt: What does the phrase "four small, stubborn hands" suggest about the milk jugs?
answerChoicesJson: The boat had real little hands that could grab the water.
They kept the boat floating and would not let it sink.
The jugs made the boat move quickly.
The boat was shaped like a person.

structuredChoicesJson.text: The boat had real little hands that could grab the water.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Plausible misreading: this takes the comparison literally.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: four milk jugs holding it up like four small, stubborn hands.

structuredChoicesJson.text: They kept the boat floating and would not let it sink.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: The comparison shows the jugs holding up the boat through rough water.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: I opened my eyes, it was still there — riding over the choppy water like the bumps were nothing at all, four milk jugs holding it up like four small, stubborn hands.

structuredChoicesJson.text: The jugs made the boat move quickly.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Unsupported inference: the boat is described as slow, not quick.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: Slow.

structuredChoicesJson.text: The boat was shaped like a person.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Plausible misreading: the comparison is about how the jugs support the boat, not its shape.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: It really did look like a sandwich — a flat board with four lumpy jugs for bread.

responseSpecJson.prompt: What does the phrase "four small, stubborn hands" suggest about the milk jugs?
choices: The boat had real little hands that could grab the water.
They kept the boat floating and would not let it sink.
The jugs made the boat move quickly.
The boat was shaped like a person.

structuredChoicesJson.text: The boat had real little hands that could grab the water.

The boat was shaped like a person.

items.subject: ELA
items.comprehensionKindRationale: The item asks for the directly stated detail that explains why June's boat survived rough water.
items.studentFacingPrompt: Which detail best explains why June's boat made it through the rough water?
answerChoicesJson: Mateo had won before with shark-shaped boats.
The boat drifted slowly along the creek bank.
June tested the boat many times to make sure it floated.
The crowd on the bank cheered for her slow milk-jug boat.

structuredChoicesJson.text: Mateo had won before with shark-shaped boats.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Wrong section: Mateo's past win does not explain why June's boat stayed afloat.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: I did not win because my boat was fast.

structuredChoicesJson.text: The boat drifted slowly along the creek bank.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Opposite emphasis: the boat was slow, but drifting slowly does not explain why it survived rough water.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: The tall ship caught the wind and flew across the water — until a sudden gust tipped it sideways, and its proud sail dragged it straight under.

structuredChoicesJson.text: June tested the boat many times to make sure it floated.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Correct.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: I won because it was the last one floating — because I had spent two weeks making sure it would not sink, while everyone else was busy making sure their boats would look amazing.

structuredChoicesJson.text: The crowd on the bank cheered for her slow milk-jug boat.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Too broad: the crowd cheered after the boat kept going, but cheering did not make it survive.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: Then more people joined in.

responseSpecJson.prompt: Which detail best explains why June's boat made it through the rough water?
choices: Mateo had won before with shark-shaped boats.
The boat drifted slowly along the creek bank.
June tested the boat many times to make sure it floated.
The crowd on the bank cheered for her slow milk-jug boat.

structuredChoicesJson.text: Mateo had won before with shark-shaped boats.

The crowd on the bank cheered for her slow milk-jug boat.

partA.stem: What is the best lesson June learns from the boat race?

choices.text: A boat that looks beautiful will win the race.
Careful design can matter more than flashy looks.
A fast boat will beat a slow boat.
A crowd can make a weak boat stronger.

items.subject: ELA
items.stem: For each detail, choose what the detail helps explain.
items.instructionText: Choose one column for each row.

rows.label: Four milk jugs held the boat up over the choppy water.
rows.evidenceQuote: four milk jugs holding it up like four small, stubborn hands
rows.rationale: The milk jugs helped June's boat stay on top of the water.

plausibleWrongRationales.fancy_boat_failed: This detail explains the sturdy boat, not the fancy boat that sank.

rows.label: June tested the boat eleven times to be sure it floated.
rows.evidenceQuote: Eleven times I tested that boat, and eleven times it floated.
rows.rationale: The repeated tests show why June knew her boat would keep floating.

plausibleWrongRationales.fancy_boat_failed: This detail is about June's preparation, not the tall ship.

rows.label: A sudden gust tipped the tall ship sideways, and its sail dragged it under.
rows.evidenceQuote: a sudden gust tipped it sideways, and its proud sail dragged it straight under
rows.rationale: The passage says the tall ship sank because wind tipped it and the sail pulled it under.

plausibleWrongRationales.june_boat_lasted: This detail explains the failure of a different boat, not June's boat.

responseSpecJson.stem: For each detail, choose what the detail helps explain.
responseSpecJson.instructionText: Choose one column for each row.

rows.label: Four milk jugs held the boat up over the choppy water.
June tested the boat eleven times to be sure it floated.
A sudden gust tipped the tall ship sideways, and its sail dragged it under.

partialCreditRules.rule: All three rows matched to the supported column.

No supported row matches, unanswered rows, or extra/multiple selections in a row.

scoring.scoringNotes: Full credit requires every required row and no incorrect or unanswered row.
